data.description:

To own NextDecade today, you have to believe the Rio Grande LNG buildout and carbon capture plans will ultimately justify a company that is still loss-making, heavily leveraged and pre-revenue. data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Intuit (INTU) After A 41% Year To Date Share Price Slide?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Intuit's current share price reflects its true worth, you are not alone. A closer look at the numbers can help you frame that question more clearly.
  • After reaching a last close of US$369.44, the stock has seen 7 day, 30 day, year to date and 1 year returns of 9.9% decline, 16.0% decline, 41.3% decline and 37.3% decline respectively. This may be reshaping how the market views both its potential and its risks.
  • Recent headlines around Intuit have focused on ongoing product development and competitive positioning in core areas like tax preparation and small business financial software. These updates help explain why sentiment can shift quickly, as investors weigh the long term role of these products against current pricing.
  • Intuit currently has a valuation score of 5 out of 6 on Simply Wall St's checks for being undervalued. The sections that follow will compare different valuation methods on this basis while also pointing you to an even deeper way of understanding what that score really means by the end of the article.

Find out why Intuit's -37.3% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Intuit Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected future cash flows and then discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now. It focuses on cash the company could generate for shareholders rather than short term earnings swings.

For Intuit, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about US$6.76b. Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, combining analyst estimates with its own extrapolations. In this framework, projected Free Cash Flow in 2030 is US$11.75b, with interim years stepping up through a series of analyst forecasts and then modelled estimates.

After discounting those future cash flows back to today, the model produces an estimated intrinsic value of US$776.69 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$369.44, this implies the stock trades at a 52.4% discount to that DCF estimate, which the model interprets as undervalued on this cash flow view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Intuit is undervalued by 52.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 55 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Intuit.

Approach 2: Intuit Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay for each share directly to the earnings that support that price. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger earnings growth or see less risk, and a lower P/E when growth expectations are weaker or risks feel higher.

Intuit currently trades on a P/E of 23.54x. That sits below the Software industry average of 28.05x and well below the peer group average of 44.75x. On the surface, this gap can suggest a different mix of growth expectations and perceived risk compared with other Software names.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Intuit is 33.81x. This is its proprietary view of what a “normal” P/E could look like once factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks are considered together. This tailored Fair Ratio can be more helpful than a simple industry or peer comparison because it adjusts for Intuit’s own profile rather than assuming all Software companies deserve similar multiples. With the current P/E at 23.54x and the Fair Ratio at 33.81x, the shares screen as undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives on Simply Wall St give you a simple way to write the story you believe about Intuit, link that story to explicit forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins, and then see the Fair Value that falls out of those assumptions compared with today’s price.

Think of a Narrative as your personal Intuit thesis written in numbers and words together. In it, you spell out why the business might grow or slow, attach that view to a detailed forecast, and let the platform convert it into a Fair Value that you can compare against the live share price to decide whether the stock currently looks expensive or cheap to you.

These Narratives live inside the Simply Wall St Community page, which is used by millions of investors. data.title: Burkhalter Holding (SWX:BRKN) Margin Uptick To 5.1% Tests Concerns Over Slowing Earnings Growth
data.description:
Burkhalter Holding (SWX:BRKN) has opened FY 2025 with first half revenue of CHF 559.0 million and basic EPS of CHF 2.26, providing a new set of figures for investors to compare with its recent track record. Over the past few reporting periods, revenue changed from CHF 526.9 million and EPS of CHF 2.19 in 1H 2024 to CHF 629.7 million and EPS of CHF 3.20 in 2H 2024. Trailing 12 month EPS was most recently CHF 5.78 on revenue of about CHF 1.2b, giving context to a business where profitability and margins are now under close scrutiny.

See our full analysis for Burkhalter Holding.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to set these results against the prevailing market stories about Burkhalter Holding and see which narratives hold up and which need a rethink.

Five year growth vs recent 7.3% pace

  • Earnings have grown 24.1% per year over the past five years, while trailing twelve month earnings growth sits at 7.3%, so the recent pace is slower than the longer term record.
  • What stands out for the more bullish view is that this slower 7.3% trailing growth still sits on top of a long run of 24.1% annual earnings growth. At the same time, the most recent half year net income of CHF 23.97 million is close to the CHF 23.30 million from 1H 2024, which means investors who expect the historic pace to continue need to keep an eye on how future halves compare to the CHF 61.34 million trailing net income.

Margins edge up to 5.1%

  • Net profit margin in the trailing twelve months is 5.1%, slightly above the 4.9% level cited for the prior year, on trailing revenue of CHF 1.21b and net income of CHF 61.34 million.
  • Supporters of a more optimistic angle argue that steady profitability is important, and the move from a 4.9% margin to 5.1% alongside trailing EPS of CHF 5.78 and half year net income holding around CHF 24.0 million provides some backing for that. However, revenue in 1H 2025 of CHF 559.0 million sits below the CHF 629.7 million recorded in 2H 2024, which shows that stronger margins are working against a softer recent revenue period rather than riding on top of broad based growth.

Mixed signals from P/E and DCF fair value

  • Critics who are more bearish on the valuation argue that paying CHF 179.60 when the DCF fair value is CHF 104.75 and earnings are forecast to grow at about 6.45% per year with revenue around 1.9% a year leaves less room for positive surprise. That concern is reinforced by the description of the dividend track record as unstable, which matters for anyone expecting a smoother income profile from a stock trading above its modelled cash flow value.

data.description:

AAR scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: AAR Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model looks at the cash AAR is expected to generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to a single value today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all those future cash flows are worth in today’s dollars.

For AAR, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow (FCF) is about $47.8 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections used in this model show FCF rising to $129.0 million by 2030, with a series of annual estimates between 2026 and 2035 that are discounted back using the 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. All cash flows in this model are in $.

Bringing those projected cash flows back to today, the DCF output suggests an intrinsic value of about $59.56 per share, compared with the recent share price of $123.77. That implies the shares screen as around 107.8% above this particular intrinsic value estimate, so in this model the stock appears expensive at current levels.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for AAR.

Approach 2: AAR Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like AAR, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that support that price. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk in those earnings, and look for a lower P/E when growth expectations are more modest or risks feel higher.

AAR currently trades on a P/E of 28.4x. That sits below the Aerospace & Defense industry average P/E of 38.9x, and well below the peer group average of 86.8x. On the surface, that might suggest a lower valuation than many peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for AAR is 24.7x. This is a proprietary P/E estimate that reflects factors such as AAR’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk characteristics. data.description:

Approach 1: Enovis Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model looks at the cash Enovis is expected to generate in the future and discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the entire business might be worth right now.

For Enovis, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $31.8 million. Analysts provide explicit estimates out to 2028, where free cash flow is projected at $166 million. Simply Wall St then extrapolates further, with ten year projections reaching about $240.9 million by 2035.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of around $43.75 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about $24.86, the DCF output suggests the stock is trading at a 43.2% discount to this estimate. This indicates that Enovis appears undervalued on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Enovis Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings can be noisy or negative, the P/S ratio is often a useful yardstick because it compares the share price with the revenue the business is already generating. Investors tend to pay higher or lower P/S multiples depending on what they expect for future growth and how risky they think those revenues are.

Enovis currently trades on a P/S of 0.64x. This sits well below the Medical Equipment industry average P/S of 2.68x and the peer group average of 6.51x. On a simple comparison with those benchmarks, the shares look inexpensive relative to sales.
